works.t: Strike Up the Band: The Man I Love

concerts.label: Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio
Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: They started pulling it out after Woody left for the night, like the last set Sam Marowitz used to call tunes and they started calling that, and they really liked playing it and Woody recorded it later.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack Palmer
about_quotes.evidence: That was a heck of a band, yeah. Pete Candoli on trumpet, Sam Marowitz on saxophone, Flip Phillips on sax, tenor, Sam played lead.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack Palmer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 24, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: I love Sam Marowitz, don’t get me wrong, but they were looking for a little different stuff to serve the music.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods interviewed by Monk Rowe, Delaware Water Gap, PA, November 8, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Dave McKenna
about_quotes.evidence: Sam Marowitz was with the band Bop City when it opened but he didn’t stay
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave McKenna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Woody Herman
about_quotes.evidence: We would (unintelligible) saxophone sections, which we did. Sam Marowitz came back... And then when Getz and Zoot and -- and Sam had tried to hold these guys together as far as the section was concerned
